title: Gyárfás–Sumner Conjecture (Conjecture 1.1)
context_text: A graph $H$ is called $\chi$-bounding if there exists a function $f$ such that $\chi(G) \leq f(\omega(G))$ for every graph $G$ containing no induced copy of $H$. Every $\chi$-bounding graph must be a forest; the Gyárfás–Sumner conjecture asserts the converse. Prior to this paper, the conjecture had been verified only for trees whose high-degree vertices are clustered closely together (trees of radius at most two, certain radius-three trees, and subdivided stars).
paper_contribution: Proves the conjecture for two new families of trees whose high-degree vertices may be arbitrarily far apart: (i) trees obtained by adding one vertex to a subdivided star (a family containing all two-legged caterpillars), and (ii) trees obtained from a subdivided star and a star by joining their centres with a path (a family containing all double brooms); both follow from the main result that for all $\kappa \geq 0$ and $k, d \geq 1$ there exists $c \geq 0$ such that every graph $G$ with $\omega(G) \leq \kappa$ and $\chi(G) > c$ is $(k,d)$-starry.
notes: PDF extraction; the body of the proof text is intact but the bibliography and later sections are replaced by a list of arXiv IDs, so reference years are estimated from standard literature.
paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. XII. Distant stars
